description:
You are acting as a database administrator who has been asked to begin optimising the SQL workload that runs inside the organisation’s
    PostgreSQL container.  
    Your first step is to identify **which** of the currently-running containers is using the most CPU time so you know where to start
    looking for problematic queries.

    The operations team has already saved a one–shot copy of 
    “docker stats --no-stream” output in the file shown below.  
    ‑ Path:  /home/user/run/docker_stats_sample.log  

    Your job:
    1. From that file, determine the single container that is consuming the highest percentage of CPU.  
       •  Only the very first data column after the header line (“CONTAINER ID”) must be ignored;  
          you must pick the container **NAME** (second column) which has the numerically-largest value
          in the “CPU %” column.  
       •  In case two containers have exactly the same CPU %, choose the one that appears first in the file    
         (this rule lets the automated tests stay deterministic).

    2. Write the name of that container, in lowercase, to the file
           /home/user/optimization/top_cpu_container.log  
       •  The output file must contain **only** the container name followed by a single newline character.  
         Example:  
           pg_db\n  
       •  Nothing else (no extra spaces, headers or diagnostics) may be present in that file.

    3. You must accomplish the whole task with exactly one terminal command
       (command chaining with pipes, substitutions, redirects, etc. is allowed,
        but everything must run as one command issued on a single shell prompt).

    The automated test will validate:
    •  that /home/user/optimization/top_cpu_container.log exists;  
    •  that its contents are exactly one line, consisting solely of the winning
       container’s name in lowercase followed by a newline.

    Feel free to inspect /home/user/run/docker_stats_sample.log in the terminal,
    but remember that only one command should actually write the final answer file.
truth:
<!--
      Pre-existing file that the agent will read
    -->
    <file path="/home/user/run/docker_stats_sample.log"><![CDATA[
CONTAINER ID   NAME              CPU %     MEM USAGE / LIMIT     MEM %     NET I/O           BLOCK I/O         PIDS
bf27be1234ab   pg_db             72.10%    256MiB / 2GiB          12.5%     1.2GB / 980MB     40MB / 15MB       35
66beccaa9a22   redis_cache       1.23%     20MiB / 2GiB           0.9%      120MB / 80MB      5MB / 4MB         6
a9c8f312654b   nginx_web         0.05%     10MiB / 2GiB           0.5%      500MB / 300MB     1MB / 1MB         3
    ]]></file>

    <!--
      File that **must** be created by the agent
    -->
    <expected_output path="/home/user/optimization/top_cpu_container.log">pg_db\n</expected_output>
name: task_000000_d54afd51
